coredata.dc:title: High-efficiency laminated luminescent solar concentrators based on carbon dots and nanorings

coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Danzhou
Hua, Zhiwen
Wang, Xinchu
Yan, Jiaheng
Xin, Wei

Wang, Xianbao
Wei, Lai
Wang, Jianying
coredata.dc:description: Creation of novel fluorescent nanomaterials with high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) will lead to high-efficiency luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs). Herein, two kinds of carbon dots (CDs), e.g., blue and green emissive CDs and one novel carbon nanorings (NRs) with yellow emission are synthesized by the solvothermal method. Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid is used as one main precursor to create blue, green emission carbon dots (B/GCDs) and yellow emission carbon nanorings (YCNRs). Importantly, the PLQYs of B/GCDs and YCNRs are 59.8±3.2 %, 12.2±0.7 % and 63.2±1.5 %, respectively. Moreover, B/GCDs and YCNRs are used as fluorescent nanomaterials to construct laminated LSCs with single-layer and double-layer through one-step photopolymerization technique. For single-layer laminated LSCs, YCNRs-based LSCs have the maximum external optical efficiency (η opt), up to 3.05 %. Furthermore, for double-layer laminated LSCs, the LSCs based on BCDs and YCNRs achieve the highest η opt of 4.09 %. Compared to single-layer laminated LSCs, the double-layer laminated LSCs show an increase of 34 % in η opt. Their internal quantum efficiency (η int) of 29.88 % and external quantum efficiency (η ext) of 25.18 % are obtained, respectively. The presented values are just for single-wavelength excitation. Thus, the synthesis of novel YCNRs with high PLQYs contributes to the optical performances at a large level. Remarkably, the optimal double-layer laminated LSCs show good transparency.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Carbon nanorings
LSCs
PLQYs
External optical efficiency

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs
Preparation of laminated LSCs with single-layer and double-layer
Synthesis and characterization of BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs
Optical properties and performances of single-layer laminated LSCs
Optical properties and performances of laminated LSCs with double-layer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The scheme of laminated LSCs with double-layer based on BCDs and YCNRs.
The HRTEM images of (a) BCDs, (b)GCDs and (c)YCNRs. The inset is the corresponding lattice structure.

UV absorption, PL excitation, and emission spectra of (a) BCDs, (d) GCDs and (g)YCNRs in DMF, respectively. (b, e, h) FTIR spectra of (b) BCDs, (e) GCDs and (h)YCNRs, respectively. (c, f, i) full XPS spectra of (c)BCDs, (f)GCDs and (i)YCNRs, respectively.

Photographs of (a) BCDs-based single-layer laminated LSCs with different BCDs concentrations (from left to right: 1.9–15.1 wt%), (c) GCDs-based single-layer laminated LSCs with different GCDs concentrations (from left to right: 0.6–3.0 wt%), and (e) YCNRs-based single-layer laminated LSCs with different YCNRs concentrations (from left to right: 1.6–9.6 wt%) under daylight, respectively. (b, d, f) Photographs of (a, c, e) under UV light (365 nm), respectively.

(a, b, c) UV absorption, transmission spectra and PL spectra of BCDs based single-layer laminated LSCs with different BCDs concentrations. (d, e, f) UV absorption, transmission spectra and PL spectra of single-layer laminated LSCs based on GCDs at different concentrations of GCDs. (g, h, i) UV absorption, transmission spectra and PL spectra of single-layer laminated LSCs based on YCNRs at different concentrations of YCNRs.

Relationship between photocurrent density and photovoltage (J-V) for single-layer laminated LSCs (25 × 25 × 2 mm3) based on (a) BCDs, (b) GCDs, and (c) YCNRs, respectively. The corresponding external optical efficiency histograms for (d) BCDs, (e) GCDs, and (f) YCNRs-based single-layer laminated LSCs (25 × 25 × 2 mm3).

(a) Photographs of laminated LSCs with double-layer (from left to right: BCDs/YCNRs, GCDs/YCNRs, BCDs/GCDs) under daylight and UV light (365 nm) irradiation, respectively. (b) Emission spectra of double-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs/YCNRs. (c) UV absorption spectra of laminated LSCs with double-layer. (d) Transmission spectra of laminated LSCs with double-layer. (e) Relationship between photocurrent density and photovoltage (J-V) of laminated LSCs with double-layer. (f) Corresponding external optical efficiencies of laminated LSCs with double-layer.

head.ce:title: High-efficiency laminated luminescent solar concentrators based on carbon dots and nanorings

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Green Preparation and Application of Functional Materials, Ministry of Education, Collaborative Innovation Center for Advanced Organic Chemical Materials Co-constructed by the Province and Ministry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Polymer Materials, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Hubei University

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute to this work equally

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Creation of novel fluorescent nanomaterials with high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) will lead to high-efficiency luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs). Herein, two kinds of carbon dots (CDs), e.g., blue and green emissive CDs and one novel carbon nanorings (NRs) with yellow emission are synthesized by the solvothermal method. Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid is used as one main precursor to create blue, green emission carbon dots (B/GCDs) and yellow emission carbon nanorings (YCNRs). Importantly, the PLQYs of B/GCDs and YCNRs are 59.8±3.2 %, 12.2±0.7 % and 63.2±1.5 %, respectively. Moreover, B/GCDs and YCNRs are used as fluorescent nanomaterials to construct laminated LSCs with single-layer and double-layer through one-step photopolymerization technique. For single-layer laminated LSCs, YCNRs-based LSCs have the maximum external optical efficiency (η opt), up to 3.05 %. Furthermore, for double-layer laminated LSCs, the LSCs based on BCDs and YCNRs achieve the highest η opt of 4.09 %. Compared to single-layer laminated LSCs, the double-layer laminated LSCs show an increase of 34 % in η opt. Their internal quantum efficiency (η int) of 29.88 % and external quantum efficiency (η ext) of 25.18 % are obtained, respectively. The presented values are just for single-wavelength excitation. Thus, the synthesis of novel YCNRs with high PLQYs contributes to the optical performances at a large level. Remarkably, the optimal double-layer laminated LSCs show good transparency.

ce:section.ce:para: Conventional semitransparent solar cells, including Si-based or CdTe-based solar cells, have been widely used in building integrated photovoltaics (BIPVs), such as solar windows, roofs, and facades, however, they suffer from high cost, complicated technologies, and more accessories. To solve the above issues, luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) have been becoming one potential candidate for application of BIPVs owing to their advantages, such as low cost, tunable transparency, easy architecture and fabrication, tunable color, flexibility, and use of diffuse light [1–7]. LSCs are one new kind of large-area sunlight harvesting and concentrating devices [8]. LSCs typically consist of transparent thin polymer slab and fluorophores, such as quantum dots (QDs) and dyes. Fluorophores can be coated or embedded on/in the transparent thin polymer slabs. Conventional Si-based PV cells can further be mounted at the edge of thin polymer slabs [9,10]. Once sunlight irradiates the LSC surface, the fluorophores inside LSCs will absorb one wavelength of sunlight and reemit another wavelength of light. Furthermore, the other wavelength of light will be waveguided to the edge of LSC owing to the total internal reflection (TIR) effect [7,11,12]. The light at the LSC edge will further be converted into electricity by the solar cell. To sum up, the LSC can by itself absorb solar light, while its role is solely that of harvesting and concentrating sunlight over the edge of the slab. A solar cell connected to the LSC is needed to produce electricity. LSCs with high performances are highly desired to meet practical applications (normally 5 % of power conversion efficiency (PCE)) [13,14].

LSC performances, e.g., power conversion efficiencies (η PCE) and external optical efficiency (η opt) mainly depend on the fluorescent materials and polymer waveguide slab. Among them, the creation of novel fluorescent materials is one critical factor for realizing efficient LSCs. Up to now, various fluorophores, including organic dyes [15,16], metal nanoclusters [17], semiconductor QDs [18–21], aggregation induced emission molecules[22–26], perovskite QDs[27–29], and carbon dots (CDs) [30–33], lanthanide-based fluorescent materials [34] have been widely used in LSCs. Although dyes or conventional QDs have benefits including high absorption coefficients and tunable absorption/emission spectra [35], they suffer high cost, weak photostability, and toxicity, which is not favorable to the environment and LSC fabrication [36]. However, rhodamines are highly stable. By comparison, CDs pose great potential in high-efficiency LSC due to their exceptional features, including low cost, high PLQYs, non-toxicity, environmental friendliness, ease of synthesis, fluorescence tunability, and good photostability [37,38]. In 2017, blue-emitting N-doped CDs (N-CDs) were first synthesized by Gong et al., which was further embedded into PMMA thin film slab, forming single-layer LSCs with the η opt of 4.75 % [39]. Subsequently, Zhao et al., synthesized green emissive CDs with PLQY of 65 %, and realized the fabrication of single-layer large-area LSCs (100–225 cm2) with the η opt of 2.2 %. Chen et al., reported the single-layer LSC (5×5 cm2) with an η opt of 3.1 % by using yellow-color solid state CDs with the PLQY of 98 % [37]. Recently, one new type of CD, a boric acid-functionalized graphene QDs (GQDs) have been created and used to prepare single-layer large area LSC (10×10 cm2), η PCE of 1.4 % was obtained [40]. To further improve the LSC efficiency, tandem LSCs by combining two or more CDs are developed along with the creation of novel CDs [41,42]. For example, red and green CDs were designed and used as fluorescent materials to create tandem LSCs. The red CDs have a broad absorption from 300 to 650 nm and a PLQY of ∼35 % in ethanol, leading to η PCE of 2.3 %[43]. Similarly, a tandem large area (∼100 cm2) LSCs with yellow and red emitting CDs had been reported [44]. Otherwise, multicolor-emitting CDs were used to construct tandem LSCs which had an external quantum efficiency of 2.3 % [45]. Our group also synthesized multicolor-emitting (red, yellow, green) CDs by solvothermal synthesis of phenylenediamine (PD) isomers (p-, o-, m-PDs) [42]. The PLQYs of red, yellow, and green CDs were 15.01, 41.52, and 7.60 %, respectively, and the η opt of 4.03 % in tandem LSC based on red, yellow, and green CDs was obtained. On the other hand, tandem LSCs by combining CDs with other fluorescent materials, such as dyes[15], AIE molecules [26], CdSe/CdS QDs [46], and perovskite QDs [47], have also been explored to further enhancing the performances of LSCs. However, there still exists toxicity and photostability issues. Thus, to date, it is still a challenge to achieve eco-friendly high-efficiency LSCs. Based on CD’s architecture, the design and synthesis of novel carbon fluorescent nanomaterials with high PLQYs will facilitate the development of eco-friendly and high-efficiency LSCs.

In this work, inspired by our previous work[42] and to address the above important issues, two new types of CDs, e.g., blue and green emissive CDs (B/GCDs) and one novel yellow emitting carbon nanorings (YCNRs) with high PLQYs have been developed by one-step solvothermal method. Importantly, the B/GCDs and YCNRs have been used to prepare eco-friendly laminated LSCs with high performances (such as high efficiencies and high transparency). To the best of our knowledge, it is the first report that YCNRs have been used to construct high-efficiency LSCs. The B/GCDs and YCNRs show high PLQYs as high as 59.8±3.2 %, 12.2±0.7 % and 63.2±1.5 %, respectively. Subsequently, single-layer or double-layer laminated LSCs have been constructed by using B/GCDs and YCNRs as fluorescent nanomaterials through a one-step photopolymerization technique. Among single-layer laminated LSCs, YCNRs-based single-layer LSCs have the maximum η opt of 3.05 %. Furthermore, for double-layer laminated LSCs, the double-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs and YCNRs emit blue and yellow light covering almost the whole visible range, as shown in Fig. 1, resulting in the highest η opt of 4.09 %, an increase of 34 % in η opt compared to that of YCNRs based single-layer laminated LSCs. Correspondingly, the double-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs and YCNRs also achieve high internal quantum efficiency (η int) of 29.88 % and external quantum efficiency (η ext) of 25.18 %, respectively. The presented values are just for single-wavelength excitation. Importantly, the transmittance of the optimal double-layer laminated LSCs is above 40 % over 450 nm, and the transparency increases to 80 % above 580 nm of light wavelength. Thus, the synthesis of novel YCNRs with high PLQYs plays an important role in the optical performances. Otherwise, the double-layer laminated structure strategy broadens the range of emitted light, effectively simulating sunlight and improving the efficiency and stability of LSCs, accelerating the practical application of LSCs in BIPVs.

Hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA, 96 %), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA, 98 %), 4-Di-p-tolylaminobenzaldehyde (98 %), 3,4,9,10-perylenetetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA, 98 %), and 2,4-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (2,4-DBSA, 98 %) were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. 3,4-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (3,4-DBSA, 98 %) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. 2-hydroxy-2-methyl-1-phenyl-1-propanone (HMPP, 98 %) was obtained from Adamas-beta (Shanghai, China). Ethanol (EtOH, 99.7 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, AR), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, AR), and polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG 400, CP) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (China). The reagents mentioned above were all utilized directly, without further purification. The clear glass substrates with sizes of 25 × 25× 0.3, 25 × 25× 1.0, and 25 × 25× 1.5 mm3, respectively are supplied by Gulo Glass Co., Ltd.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of BCDs: 60 mg of 3,4-DBSA and 40 mg of 4-Di-p-tolylaminobenzaldehyde were first added in 10 mL of DMF. Then, 200 μL of HCl and 3 mL of PEG 400 were added to the above solution, and a homogeneous mixed solution was obtained after stirring. The mixed solution was transfered into a 50 mL stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 12 h. After the temperature was cooled to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 10 min to remove the larger precipitates. The supernatant was collected and purified by silica column chromatography. Finally, the purified BCDs were obtained after removing solvents under vacuum for further use.

Synthesis of GCDs: 100 mg of 2,4-DBSA was first added in 10 mL of EtOH, then 150 μL of HCl was dissolved into the above solution and stirred to obtain a homogeneous mixed solution. The mixed solution was transfered into a 50 mL stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave which was placed in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The post-treatment is the same as that of BCDs.

Synthesis of YCNRs: 60 mg of 3,4-DBSA and 40 mg of PTCDA were added in 10 mL of DMF, then, 200 μL of HCl and 3 mL of PEG 400 were added into the above solution and stirred to obtain a homogeneous mixed solution. The mixed solution was transfered into a 50 mL stainless steel Teflon-lined autoclave and reaction condition at 200 °C for 12 h was used. The post-treatment is the same as that of BCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of laminated LSCs with single-layer and double-layer
ce:section.ce:para: Preparation of laminated LSCs with single-layer: a certain amount of BCDs was first added into 1 mL of HEMA to prepare BCDs-HEMA suspensions with different BCD concentrations. Following that, 0.05 mL of HMPP and 0.05 mL of EGDMA were mixed with the above suspension, and a homogeneous solution was formed. 150 μL of the above solution was dropped onto a transparent glass sheet and covered with another glass sheet of the same size. The above sandwich was then photopolymerized under 365 nm UV light to make laminated LSCs with single-layer based on BCDs. Laminated LSCs with single-layer based on GCDs or YCNRs were prepared in the same way as described above.

Preparation of laminated LSCs with double-layer: For double-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs and YCNRs, a certain amount of YCNRs were first added into 1 mL of HEMA to prepare YCNRs-HEMA suspensions. After that, 0.05 mL of HMPP and 0.05 mL of EGDMA were mixed with the above suspension, and a homogeneous solution was formed. 150 μL of the above solution was dropped onto a transparent glass sheet and covered with another glass sheet of the same size. The above sandwich was then photopolymerized under UV light to obtain laminated LSCs with single-layer based on YCNRs. After that, BCDs were added to 1 mL of HEMA to prepare BCDs-HEMA suspensions. Subsequently, 0.05 mL of HMPP and 0.05 mL of EGDMA were mixed with the above suspension, and a homogeneous solution was formed. 150 μL of the above solution was dropped onto a laminated LSCs with single-layer based on YCNRs and covered with another glass sheet of the same size, which was then photopolymerized under UV light to make a laminated LSCs with double-layer based on BCDs and YCNRs. Laminated LSCs with double-layer based on B/GCDs and GCDs/YCNRs were prepared in the same way as described above. The optimal CDs and NRs concentrations were used for the construction of double-layer laminated LSCs. The optimal concentration of BCDs is 7.5 wt%, the optimal concentration of GCDs is 1.2 wt%, and the optimal concentration of YCNRs is 6.4 wt%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, to obtain high-efficiency LSCs, two types of CDs, B/GCDs are first synthesized. Based on the CDs, one novel YCNRs are further designed and synthesized. First, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) is used to characterize the size and morphology of BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs ( Fig. 2a, b, c). From the HRTEM images in Fig. 2a, b, it can be shown that the BCDs and GCDs have a spherical structure with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the lattice spacing of the graphene (100) plane [42]. After statistical analysis, the average diameters of BCDs and GCDs are about 3.5±2.0 nm and 4.0±2.0 nm (Fig. S1a and S1b). In addition, the HRTEM image in Fig. 2c shows that the YCNRs have a distinct ring structure with a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm, corresponding to the lattice spacing of the graphene (002) plane[48]. Otherwise, the average diameter of YCNRs is about 21.3±5.0 nm (Fig. S1c).

Following that, the optical characteristics of BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs are demonstrated. First, as shown in Fig. 3a and d, the UV–vis absorption curves of the plots show absorption peaks at 356 nm for BCDs and 416 nm for GCDs, which come from the n-π* transitions of functional groups like carboxyl groups or other aromatic structures in BCDs and GCDs [27]. While the absorption peaks of YCNRs are mainly at 500 nm, as shown in Fig. 3g, this band may be attributed to the electronic transitions of various surface states in YCNRs [48]. From the emission spectra in Fig. 3a, d, and g, the emissive peaks of BCDs at 380 nm excitation are located at 446 nm, the emissive peaks of GQDs at 460 nm excitation are located at 500 nm, and the emissive peaks of YCNRs at 460 nm excitation are located at 553 nm and 580 nm, which probably come from two emitting center from CDs surfaces [49]. The Stokes shift of the B/GCDs and YCNRs is about 90 nm, 84 nm, and 53 nm, respectively, from the absorption and emission spectra in Fig. 3a, d, and g. In addition, the PL spectra (Fig. S2) show that emission peak positions of BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs at different excitation light wavelengths are almost the same, indicating BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs have the excitation independence, probably coming from the uniform surface state [37]. Fig. S3 shows the PL decay curves of BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs under 375, 420, 485 nm excitation, respectively. Further, the lifetime data are shown in Table S1, which exhibit that the PL decay curves are fitted with double exponential decay kinetics which come from different emission states of band-edge and localized electronic states [50,51]. The average lifetimes (τ ave) of BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs are 3.74, 9.44, and 3.61 ns, respectively. According to previous work[52], PLQY=Kr×τ (Kr is the radiative rate and τ is the lifetime), thus, high PLQYs can be obtained for BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs with 59.8±3.2 %, 12.2±0.7 %, and 63.2±1.5 % based on three measurements, respectively (Fig. S4).

The FTIR results show that BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs have similar chemical bond structures (Fig. 3b, e, h). The peak at 3447 cm−1 and 1664 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the -OH/-NH2 group and CN/CO, respectively. The peak at 1390 cm−1, 1096 cm−1, and 654 cm−1 is from the C-N stretching vibration, the C-O stretching vibration, and the C-S stretching vibration, respectively. In addition, XPS tests have been used to demonstrate the chemical bonding and elemental composition of the BCDs, the GCDs, and the YCNRs. The XPS results indicate the presence of carbon (C1s), oxygen (O1s) nitrogen (N1s), and sulfur (S2p) at binding energies of 284, 531, 398, and 164 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 3c, f, i. The high-resolution XPS spectra further reveal the specific details of the chemical bonds (Fig. S5). For the BCDs, the high-resolution spectra of C1s show four different peaks for C-O (285.3 eV), C-C/CC (284.5 eV), C-N (285.1 eV), and C-S (285.9 eV) (Fig. S5a). The high-resolution spectra of N1s show three different peaks for N-C2 (398.1 eV), N-C3 (399.8 eV), and -NH2 (402.1 eV) (Fig. S5d). For GCDs and YCNRs, the high-resolution spectra of C1s show five different peaks of CO (288.6 eV), C-C/CC (284.5 eV), C-N (285.1 eV), C-O (285.3 eV), and C-S (285.9 eV) (Fig. S5b and S5c). The high-resolution spectra of N1s exhibit N-C2 (398.1 eV), N-C3 (399.8 eV), and -NH2 (402.1 eV) peaks (Fig. S5e and S5f). The FIRT and XPS data confirm that the B/GCDs and YCNRs have rich carboxyl and amino groups on their surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and performances of single-layer laminated LSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs, three single-layer laminated LSCs are further prepared. First, all single-layer laminated LSCs possess excellent transparency, and the bottom words can be seen under sunlight irradiation ( Fig. 4a, c, e). Second, under the excitation of a 365 nm UV lamp, BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs-based single-layer laminated LSCs show uniform blue, green, and yellow emission, exhibiting the uniform distribution of CD and NRs in polymer slab, as shown in Fig. 4b, d, f. The three laminated LSC edges show intense PL emission, resulting from the waveguide effect which fluorescence emission is guided to the LSC edge.

The absorption properties of LSCs are first tested to further demonstrate the optical properties. As shown in Fig. 5a, d, g, the absorption ability of all the single-layer laminated LSCs increases with the increased CDs or NRs concentrations. While, with the increased CDs or NRs concentrations the transparency of LSCs decreases (Fig. 5b, e, h). In detail, BCDs-based single-layer laminated LSCs almost maintain 80 % transmittance over 500 nm of light wavelength with increased BCD concentrations. While, the transparency of GCDs-based single-layer laminated LSCs can reach 60 % over 500 nm of light wavelength when GCDs concentration increases from 0.6 to 3.0 wt%. Similarly, the transparency of YCNRs-based single-layer laminated LSCs at 500 nm decreases from 70 % to 13 % when the concentration of YCNRs increases from 1.6 wt% to 9.6 wt%. However, all the single-layer laminated LSCs can achieve more than 80 % transparency at 580–800 nm, maintaining excellent transparency. The PL emissive intensities of single-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs all show a first increasing and then decreasing trend with increasing CDs or NRs concentrations, as shown in Fig. 5c, f, i. The PL emission intensities reach the maximum values at 7.5 wt% of BCDs, 1.2 wt% of GCDs, and 6.4 wt% of YCNRs concentrations, respectively. Thus, there exist optimal CDs or NRs concentrations for realizing high-efficiency LSCs. Otherwise, according to the previous report[53], the AVT, CCT, and CRI of single-layer optimal laminated LSCs based on BCDs are 88.55 %, 5513 K, and 93.68, respectively. The AVT, CCT, and CRI of single-layer optimal laminated LSCs based on GCDs are 82.72 %, 5513k, and 91.24, respectively. The AVT, CCT, and CRI of single-layer optimal laminated LSCs based on YCNRs are 70.2 %, 5513 K, and 70.72, respectively. The decrease of PL emission intensities at high CD or NR concentrations may be due to the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect and self-absorption effect which come from the red-shift of emission spectra[54,55]. According to the previous report[56], the re-absorption effect has been evaluated. The overlap integrals (OI) of single-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs are 5.16, 5.8, 7.64, 10.03, and 10.87 when the BCDs concentration increases from 1.9 wt% to 15.1 wt%, respectively, as shown in Table S2. The OI of single-layer laminated LSCs based on GCDs are 5.12, 10.14, 15.45, 17.76, and 19.33 when the GCDs concentration increases from 0.6 wt% to 3.0 wt%, respectively, as shown in Table S3. The OI of single-layer laminated LSCs based on YCNRs are 11.04, 18.7, 23.88, 26.82, and 33.39 when the YCNRs concentration increases from 1.6 wt% to 9.6 wt%, respectively, as shown in Table S4. The results indicate that the OI increases with the increased concentrations of BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs, further leading to the increase of self-absorption and decrease of PL intensity. It is noted that the Stokes shift of single-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs are 120 nm, 70 nm, and 54 nm, respectively.

To achieve high-performance LSCs, the J-V characteristic curves of all single-layer laminated LSCs were tested, as shown in Fig. 6a, b, and c, showing the relationship between CDs or NRs concentrations and J-V characteristic curves. It is noted that the LSC can by itself absorb solar light, while its role is solely that of harvesting and concentrating sunlight over the edge of the slab. A solar cell connected to the LSC is needed to produce electricity. The results show that the J-V characteristic curves of all single-layer laminated LSCs coincide with the variation of PL emission intensities, and all of them show a first increasing and then decreasing trend. Based on this, the external optical efficiency (η opt) is further obtained according to Eq. (1) [57]:
(1)
η
opt
=
I
LSC
/ (
I
SC
× G )
where ILSC is the short circuit current from a solar cell attached to the LSC and ISC is the short circuit current measured for the same solar cell under direct illumination with the same light source. G is the ratio of the top area (Atop) to the edge area (Aedge) of the LSCs. As shown in Fig. 6d, for BCDs-based single-layer laminated LSCs, the η opt are 2.14 %, 2.53 %, 2.95 %, 2.79 %, and 2.67 % as the concentrations increase from 1.9 to 15.1 wt%, respectively. Similarly, as the concentrations of GCDs increase from 0.6 to 3.0 wt%, the η opt of GCDs-based single-layer laminated LSCs are 1.37 %, 1.52 %, 1.45 %, 1.31 %, and 1.29 %, respectively. As the concentrations of YCNRs increase from 1.6 to 9.6 wt%, the η opt of YCNRs-based single-layer laminated LSCs are 2.38 %, 2.94 %, 3.05 %, 2.92 %, and 2.89 %, respectively (Fig. 6e, f). The results show that the single-layer LSCs based on BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs achieve maximum η opt at concentrations of 7.5 wt%, 1.2 wt%, and 6.4 wt%, respectively, which coincide with the optimal PL emission intensities. The detailed photovoltaic parameters are listed in Table S5. The power conversion efficiency (PCE) of single-layer optimal LSCs based on BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs is 0.26, 0.11, and 0.26 respectively. In addition, to further evaluate the optical efficiency, the internal quantum efficiency (η int) and external quantum efficiency (η ext) of the LSCs are measured. The detailed procedure is shown in the Supporting Information. The PLQY (η PL) of single-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs (7.5 wt%), GCDs (1.2 wt%), and YCNRs (6.4 wt%) are calculated as 98.65 %, 47.58 %, and 58.11 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. S6. The η edge of single-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs are 52.16 %, 39.68 %, and 46.71 %, respectively, as shown in Fig. S7. The η int can be obtained according to Eq. (2) [57], which is defined as the ratio between the number of photons emitted from the edges of LSCs and the number of incident photons absorbed:
(2)
η
int
=
η
PL
×
η
edge

The η int of single-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs are 51.46 %, 18.88 %, and 27.14 %, respectively. Furthermore, the η ext can be obtained according to Eq. (3) [57], which is defined as the ratio between the number of photons emitted from the edges of LSCs and the total number of incident photons:
(3)
η
ext
=
η
int
×
η
abs
where η abs is the absorbance of LSCs at a specific wavelength, which can be calculated by the formula: η abs = (1-R) × (1–10-OD) (OD is the optical density at a specific wavelength and R is the reflectance (Fig. S8)). Thus, the η abs of single-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs, GCDs, and YCNRs are 82.18 %, 41.12 %, and 21.96 %, respectively. The η ext was calculated to be 42.29 %, 7.76 %, and 5.96 %, respectively. By comparison, single-layer optimal laminated LSCs based on BCDs show the largest η int, and η ext, which probably come from the largest η abs. The detailed optical efficiencies are summarized in Table S6. It is noted that one incident laser light with 405 nm wavelength is used for measuring η int and η ext and the presented values are just for single-wavelength excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and performances of laminated LSCs with double-layer
ce:section.ce:para: Based on single-layer laminated LSCs, laminated LSCs with double-layer are prepared to further improve optical efficiency. Herein, the optimal concentrations, e.g., 7.5 wt% of BCDs, 1.2 wt% of GCDs, and 6.4 wt% of YCNRs have been used to prepare three laminated LSCs with double-layer. As shown in Fig. 7a, three laminated LSCs with double-layer exhibit excellent transparency under sunlight, and the words printed on the bottom paper could be clearly seen. Under the excitation of a 365 nm UV lamp, all three laminated LSCs with double-layer exhibit intense PL emission at LSC edges. In further, the edge PL emission of the laminated LSCs with double-layer based on BCDs and YCNRs are tested, as shown in Fig. 7b. The results show that the edge emission of the laminated LSCs with double-layer covers the wavelength range from 400 nm to 800 nm. Compared with laminated LSCs with single-layer, laminated LSCs with double-layer have stronger absorption, as shown in Figs. 7c and 7d. Above 580 nm, the transmittance of all laminated LSCs with double-layer reaches 80 %. It is also noted that double-layer laminated LSC based on BCDs and YCNRs can maintain ∼40 % transparency over 450 nm of light wavelength. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 7e, the J-V curves of three laminated LSCs with double-layer are tested. The results show that the current density of the laminated LSCs with double layers based on BCDs/YCNRs is the highest, reaching 3.97 mA/cm2. Correspondingly, as shown in Fig. 7f and Table S7, the laminated LSCs with double-layer have 2.26 %, 4.09 %, 2.79 % of η opt for BCDs/GCDs, BCDs/YCNRs, GCDs/YCNRs based LSCs, respectively. The η opt of the double-layer laminated larger LSCs (50×50×2 mm3) based on the BCDs/GCDs, BCDs/YCNRs and GCDs/YCNRs are 0.92 %, 1.58 % and 0.99 %, respectively (Fig. S9). Thus, BCDs/YCNRs based double-layer laminated LSC have the maximum η opt, probably coming from the high PLQYs of BCDs/YCNRs and the ability to simulate the sunlight spectrum. The η opt of the laminated LSCs with double-layer based on BCDs/YCNRs is 34 % higher than that of the single-layer LSCs based on YCNRs. On the other hand, the η int of 29.88 % and η ext of 25.18 % are obtained for the laminated LSCs with double-layer based on BCDs/YCNRs, respectively (Table S8). Therefore, the creation of novel YCNRs with high PLQYs and efficient simulation of sunlight spectrum by laminated LSCs with double-layer greatly improve the performances of LSCs.

In summary, we have synthesized two types of carbon dots (CDs), e.g., blue and green emissive CDs, and one novel yellow emitting carbon nanorings (NRs) by the solvothermal method. The B/GCDs and YCNRs have high PLQYs of 59.8±3.2 %, 12.2±0.7 %, and 63.2±1.5 %, respectively. Furthermore, we construct laminated LSCs with single-layer and double-layer through a one-step photopolymerization technique based on B/GCDs and YCNRs as fluorescent nanomaterials. Compared to single-layer laminated LSCs based on YCNRs (3.05 %), double-layer laminated LSCs based on BCDs and YCNRs have a higher η opt of 4.09 %, showing an increase of 34 %. Their internal quantum efficiency (η int) of 29.88 % and external quantum efficiency (η ext) of 25.18 % are obtained, respectively. The presented values are just for single-wavelength excitation. Remarkably, the optimal double-layer laminated LSCs maintain good transparency. Thus, the synthesis of novel fluorescent nanomaterials, e.g., YCNRs with high PLQYs plays an important role in optical performances and poses great potential in the practical application of LSCs.

Danzhou Huang, Zhiwen Hua: Investigation, Validation, Data curation, Writing-original draft. Xinchu Wang, Jiaheng Yan: Formal analysis. Wei Xin: Investigation. Xianbao Wang: Supervision. Jinhua Li, Lai Wei: Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jianying Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Knowledge Innovation Program of Wuhan Science and Technology Bureau (Grant No. 2023020201010139), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Organosilicon Chemistry and Material Technology of Ministry of Education, Hangzhou Normal University (KFJJ2022008), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51673060, 11574075), Science and Technology Department of Hubei province (Grant No. 2020BIB020), and the Ministry of Science, Overseas Expertise Introduction Center for Discipline Innovation (D18025), the Opening Project of Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Chemical Materials and Devices of Ministry of Education, Jianghan University (JDGD-202205).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nxmate.2024.100277.
